30-24  January 9, 2024  R E S O L U T I O N  WHEREAS , in   early   December 8 th   202 3 , the Township ’ s Sewer Division determined the   8-inch  clay   sanitary   main   pipe   located   behind   No.   10   Burnett   Terrace   within   the   Township ’ s   5-foot   Right-of-  Way  sewer   easement revealed   the sewer line had collapsed;   and  WHEREAS , to avoid any additional collapse of the   sewer pipe and   compromise the structural  integrity   of   the   wall   within   the   vicinity   of   the   sanitary   sewer   main ,   the   Engineering   D epartment   decided  to  replace the sewer pipe,   and   restore   ground area;   and  WHEREAS , pursuant to the Local Public Contracts Law, Emergency Contracts, N.J.S.A.  40A:11.6a the   Assistant   Municipal Engineer on   December 11 , 2023   notified the   Administration and  Purchasing Agent of the need to perform an emergency contract for this work with a   total   estimated cost  of $ 14 ,000.00 ; and  WHEREAS,   it   was   concluded that to expedite the work and to perform the emergency   repair of  the   s anitary   sewer   pipe   behind No. 10   Burnett   Terrace within the Township ’ s 5-foot Right-of-Way   to  protect   public   health   and   safety   an   emergency   contract   would   be   issued   to   Shauger   Property   Services ,  Inc. ;  and  WHEREAS,   Shauger Property Services, Inc.   submitted   a proposal   dated   December   27 , 2023 for  the emergency repair of the s anitary   sewer in the amount of $ 13,979.97 ; and  WHEREAS , the   Assistant   Municipal   Engineer by letter dated   January 3 , 202 4   recommends  paying the costs of this emergency work; and  WHEREAS , sufficient funds exist for this work; and  WHEREAS , the Township Council concurs in the foregoing .  NOW THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED   by the Township Council of the Township of West  Orange that an emergency appropriation in   an   amount   not to exceed $ 13,979.97   without authorization   is  authorized   to   Shauger   Property   Services,   Inc.   for   emergency   s anitary   sewer   construction   to   the  Township ’ s   sanitary   sewer   main   located   behind   No.   10   Burnett   Terrace   within   the   Township ’ s   5-foot  Right-of-Way.

BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED   that this work was performed pursuant to the provisions of the  Local Public   C ontracts Law, Emergency Contracts ,   N.J.S.A. 40A:11-6 as the work was required   t o  protect public health and welfar e.  Karen J. Carnevale, RMC, Municipal Clerk   Bill   Rutherford , Council President  Adopted: January 9, 2024  I certify funds are available for this work   f rom   Account No.  $ 13,979.97  John O. Gross, Chief Financial Officer

1  31-24  January 9, 2024  RESOLUTION   TO SET   THE INTE REST RA TE AND PENALTIES TO BE CHARGED  FOR DELI NQUENT TAX PAYMENTS  WHEREAS , pursuant to N.J.S.A. 43:4-67, the Township Council of the Township of West  Orange has passed a resolution fixing the rate of interest to be charged for non-payment of Tax  or Assessments; and  WHEREAS ,   the Township Council of the Township of West Orange now desires to set the  calendar year 20 24   rate   of interest and penalties to be charged taxpayers who are delinquent in  the payment of taxes and assessments   d ue and owing;  NOW,   THEREFORE   BE IT HEREBY RESOLVED   by the Township Council of the  Township of West Orange that the rate of interest and/or penalty to be charged for the non-  payment of taxes and/or assessments for when they become delinquent for the calendar year  20 24   as follows:  No interest shall be charged if payment of any installment is made within the tenth (10 th )  calendar day following the date upon which the same become payable. If the tenth day falls  on a weekend or Municipally Observed Holiday,   the grace period shall be extended until the  next working day.  Upon the expiration of the tenth (10 th ) day as aforesaid, the rate of interest shall be Eight  Percent (8%) per annum of the first $1,500.00 of said delinquency and Eighteen Percent  (18%) per annum on any amount in the excess of $1,500.00, which is to be calculated from  the date the tax was payable through and inclusive of the date of actual payment.  An additional Six percent (6%) penalty for delinquencies in excess of $10,000.00 remaining  unp a id at the end of the calendar year.  BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED   that the charges on account of delinquent properties as set  forth herein remain in effect from year to year until such time as the governing body adopts a  new resolution setting forth new or different rates .  Karen J. Carnevale , R.M.C.   B ill   Ruth erf ord  Municipal Clerk   Council President  Adopted: January 9, 2024

pages.text: 33-24  January 9, 2024  RESOLUTION AUTHORIZING THE ISSUANCE OF A  DUPLICATE TAX SALECERTIFICATE PURSUANT TO  N.J.S.A. 54:6-52.1  WHEREAS, the Tax Collector of this municipality has previously issued a tax sale  certificate to   Trevor Layne   which certificate is dated   1/18/2000   covering premises commonly  known and referred to as Block   177.08   Lot   21   as set out on the municipal tax map then in use  which certificate bears number   1085.  WHEREAS, the purchaser of the aforesaid tax sale certificate has indicated to the Tax  Collector that they have lost or otherwise misplaced the original tax sale certificate and have  duly filed the appropriate Affidavit of Loss with the Tax Collector, a copy of which is attached  hereto.  NOW THEREFORE, be it resolved by the Mayor and Governing Body of the  Municipality of the Township of West Orange that the Tax Collector of the municipality be and  is hereby authorized, upon receipt of the appropriately executed and notarized Loss Affidavit and  the payment of a fee of $ 100.00   per certificate, to issue an appropriate duplicate tax sale  certificate to the said purchaser covering the certificate lost as previously described all in  accordance with the requirements of Chapter 99 of public Laws o f 1997.  BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that a copy of this Resolution and the Loss Affidavit be  attached to the duplicate certificate to be issued to said purchased and that said duplicate  certificate shall be stamped or otherwise have imprinted upon it the word   “ Duplicate ”   as required  by law.  Karen   J. Carnevale , R.M.C.   Bill   Rutherford  Municipal Clerk   Council President  Adopted: January 9, 2024

34 - 24  January 9, 2024  R E S O L U T I O N  WHEREAS,   the Township of West Orange   requires   Engineering ,   Land Surveying   and Bid  Phase Services   for   the Main Street Corridor Crosswalks and Sidewalk Improvements ; and  WHEREAS,   the Township requires the services of a   Professional Engineering Firm; and  WHEREAS,   the Township Engineer solicited   a   proposal from   five   P rofessional   E ngineering  firm s ; a nd  WHEREAS,   CME Associates ,   responded to the Request for Proposal; and  WHEREAS,   CME Associates ,   ha ve   performed similar work for the Township on several  projects and their work quality is very good ; and  WHEREAS,   by letter   dated January 3, 2024   the Township   Assistant Municipal   Engineer  recommended a professional services contract be awarded to   CME Associates, 1460, Route 9 South,  Howell, NJ 07731   for   Professional   Engineering   and Land Surveying   Services   associated   with the  Main Street Corridor Crosswalks and sidewalks Improvements project ;   and  WHEREAS ,   CME Associates   responded to the Township’s   Request for Qualifications for  Professional Services for 202 4 ; and  WHEREAS,   sufficient funds exist for the services outlined in the proposal   for the Main  Street Corridor Crosswalks and sidewalks Improvements ;   and  WHEREAS,   the Township Council concurs in the foregoing.  NOW THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED   by the Township Council of the Township of  West Orange, New Jersey that a professional services contract be awarded to   CME Associates, 1460,  Route 9 South, Howell, NJ 07731   to provide professional   engineering   and land surveying   services for  the   Main Street Corridor Crosswalks and sidewalks Improvements project   for an amount not to  exceed   $ 75 , 0 00.00 .

BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED   that a copy of this Resolution be published in the West  Orange Chronicle within ten   (10) days of its approval as required by State Statute .  Karen Carnevale, Municipal Clerk   Bill Rutherford , Council   President  Adopted: January 9, 2024  I hereby certify funds are available   from:   ACCOU NT:  $ 75 ,000.00  John Gross, Chief Financial Officer

AN EQUAL OPPORTUNITY EMPLOYER www.westorange.org  TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  25 Lakeside Avenue, West Orange, New Jersey 07052  DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC WORKS  Engineering Division  SUSAN McCARTNEY  MAYOR  ZAYIBETH CARBALLO   Tel: (973) 325 - 41 60  Assistant Municipal Engineer   Fax: (973) 669-9588  Email: Engineering@westorange.org  December 14 ,   2023  Trevor Taylor CME 379 US Route 1 South, Suite 100 Monmouth Junction, NJ, 08852 RE:   REQUEST FOR   PROPOSALS (RFP)  MAIN STREET CORRIDOR CROSSWALKS AND SIDEWALKS IMPROVEMENTS  Dear Mr.   Taylor ,  The Engineering Division of the Department of Public Works for the Township of West Orange requests proposals from firms providing both Civil and Engineering and Land Surveying Services, which are capable of preparing the necessary improvement plans, specifications and bid documents for the following Main Street Crosswalks and Sidewalks Improvements projects:  I.   Phase 1-Main Street at Valley Road/Northfield Avenue  5 Crosswalks: 1 across Valley Road, 1 across Northfield Avenue, 3 ways across Main Street. No Pavers on St. Mark’s side (north and east) Paver Sidewalks run on Northfield Avenue from I-280 ramp/Wheeler Street to Main/Valley. The sidewalks extend south on Valley Road to #325 (west side) and the entrance of the Municipal Lot (east side). On Main Street, pavers run on the south/west side from the Orange Township line to Lindsley Avenue. The project consists of: 34-24

AN EQUAL OPPORTUNITY EMPLOYER  www.westorange.org  1.   Topographic Survey and Base Mapping  A.   Establishment of centerline of existing street.  B.   Topographic survey of all trees, shrubs, walls, driveways, walks, steps, curb  ramps and other existing features within the ROW and farther where  necessary to the proposed improvements.  C.   Right of way will be shown from available Township Tax Map information.  D.   Establish horizontal control based on North American Datum 1983 (New  Jersey State Plane Coordinate System-NAD 83) and North American Vertical  Datum 1988 (NAVD 88);  E.   Benchmark(s) to be set on site for future use;  F.   Existing conditions/Survey Control plan;  2.   Civil Engineering Services:  A.   Develop three (3) color renderings;  B.   Design considerations for the removal of existing brick paver crosswalks and  sidewalks  C.   Construction of stamped asphalt crosswalks and stamp concrete sidewalk  D.   Construction of ADA compliant curb ramps  E.   Pavement striping  F.   New traffic signs as necessary  G.   Resolve existing drainage issues at 3-4 corners of the Main St/Northfield Ave  H.   Reset drainage structures, manhole castings, frames and covers as necessary.  I.   Coordinate   with the   Township Traffic Bureau and Essex County   for   a  comprehensive Traffic Control Plan  J.   Prepare construction details;  K.   Plan Set to include but not limited to Cover Sheet, Existing Conditions/Survey  Control Plan, Demolition Plan, Construction Plan, Curb Ramp Details, Soil  Erosion and Sediment Control Plan and Details, Traffic Control Plans and  Construction Details;  L.   Include four (4) meetings with the Township Officials for Kickoff/Field,  Preliminary Design, Public Presentation and Final Design;  M.   Bid Package that includes bid set of plans and construction specifications in  accordance with public bidding laws;  N.   Bidding documents (plans and specifications) are to be part of the deliverables  to the Township via AutoCAD, hard copy and PDF files.  II.   Phase 2-Main Street at Lindsley Ave, Mt. Pleasant Ave/White St, Park Ave,  Charles St, Lakeside Ave, Tory Corner Business District,  Washington St/Franklin Ave/Llewellyn Ave and Harrison Business District  Main Street at Lindsley Avenue  1 Crosswalk across Lindsley Avenue  Pavers in front of Township Hall (west side) between concrete sidewalk and curb only.  Approximately 2 feet wide.

AN EQUAL OPPORTUNITY EMPLOYER  www.westorange.org  No pavers on east side.  Main Street at Mount Pleasant Avenue/White Street  6 Crosswalks: 4 in Box, 1 additional at N.W corner traffic island, 1 across White Street.  Main Street at Charles Street  2 Crosswalks. No Pavers except for new installation on the east side at the Lofts  development.  Main Street at Lakeside Avenue  2 Crosswalks.  There are grey brick cobblestone pavers between sidewalk and curb on both sides from  Lakeside Avenue to Alden Street/Edisonia Terrace. These are different from all other  locations and were probably installed by the Edison NPS. Ask Jimmy?  Tory Corner Business District  There are keystone pavers between sidewalk and curb extending from Babcock Place (east  side) and 250 Main Street (west side) to Ashwood Terrace (east) and Park Drive N. (west)  Main Street at Washington Street/Franklin Avenue/Llewellyn Avenue  5 Paver Crosswalks, 2 at Washington Street, 2 at Llewellyn Avenue, and one at Franklin  Avenue.  There are also keystone pavers, between sidewalk and curb, extending down Washington  Street to the Orange Township line.  Main Street at Harrison Business District  Full Brick Paver sidewalks extend from Ashwood Terrace (east) and Park Drive N. (west)  to Valley Way (east) and 450 Main Street (west). They also extend up Harrison Avenue to  Cherry Street and on Mississippi Avenue to the Bus Loop/Church Driveway.  5 Crosswalks, 3 at Main Street, 2 at Harrison Avenue at Mississippi Avenue.  1.   Topographic Survey and Base Mapping  A.   Establishment of centerline of existing street.  B.   Topographic survey of all trees, shrubs, walls, driveways, walks, steps, curb  ramps and other existing features within the ROW and farther where  necessary to the proposed improvements.  C.   Right of way will be shown from available Township Tax Map information.  D.   Establish horizontal control based on North American Datum 1983 (New  Jersey State Plane Coordinate System-NAD 83) and North American Vertical  Datum 1988 (NAVD 88);  E.   Benchmark(s) to be set on site for future use;  F.   Existing conditions/Survey Control plan;

AN EQUAL OPPORTUNITY EMPLOYER  www.westorange.org  2.   Civil Engineering Services:  A.   Develop three (3) color renderings;  B.   Design considerations for the removal of existing brick paver crosswalks and  sidewalks  C.   Construction of stamped asphalt crosswalks and stamp concrete sidewalk  D.   Construction of ADA compliant curb ramps  E.   Pavement striping  F.   New traffic signs as necessary  G.   Resolve existing drainage issues at various crosswalk crossings  H.   Reset drainage structures, manhole castings, frames and covers as necessary.  I.   Coordinate   with the   Township Traffic Bureau and Essex County   for   a  comprehensive Traffic Control Plans  J.   Prepare construction details;  K.   Plan Set to include but not limited to Cover Sheet, Existing Conditions/Survey  Control Plan, Demolition Plan, Construction Plan, Curb Ramp Details, Soil  Erosion and Sediment Control Plan and Details, Traffic Control Plans and  Construction Details;  L.   Include four (4) meetings with the Township Officials for Kickoff/Field,  Preliminary Design, Public Presentation and Final Design;  M.   Bid Package that includes bid set of plans and construction specifications in  accordance with public bidding laws.  N.   Bidding documents (plans and specifications) are to be part of the deliverables  to the Township via AutoCAD, hard copy and PDF files.  Aerial maps with project areas delineated are enclosed. Township will supply  Main St. Crosswalks Construction Plans from previous project.  The cost of services shall be for a complete design and two (2) separate bid  packages including cost estimates for based bid and alternates, plans, specifications and  bid documents (twenty copies) for bidding purposes including detour plans. All plan  specifications and bid documents shall be in conformance with the latest New Jersey  Department of Transportation specifications.   Proposals shall be on a lump sum basis.  Hourly rates shall be submitted with all proposals for any additional work.  It is the intent of this office to advertise for bids as follows:  Phase I   Main Street Corridor Crosswalks and Sidewalks Improvements   advertise for  bids by Early Spring 2024 for construction to commence in the Spring of 2024.  Phase II   Main Street Corridor Crosswalks and Sidewalks Improvements   advertise  for bids by Early Summer 2024 for construction to commence in the Fall of 2024.

AN EQUAL OPPORTUNITY EMPLOYER  www.westorange.org  Proposals   shall   be   submitted   in   writing   on   or   before   10:00   A.M.   Friday ,  December 29, 2023   to the Department of Public Works, 25 Lakeside Avenue, West  Orange, NJ 07052 and shall include a specified completion date or number of days  required for completion of the work after approval is granted to proceed.   Also,  evidence of proper insurance shall be submitted for approval by the Township Attorney.  A separate proposal for construction inspection and certificates of completion for  each phase should also be included if available from your firm.  Very Truly yours,  Zayibeth Carballo  Assistant Municipal Engineer  ZYC

documents.title: RESOLUTION MAIN STREET CORRIDOR CROSSWALK AND SIDEWALK IMPROVEMENTS
GENOVA BURNS

pages.text: 35-24  January 9, 2024  RESOLUTION  WHEREAS , the Township of West Orange is a municipal corporation of the State of New Jersey, duly  established and operating as a Faulkner Act Mayor-Council form of government; and  WHEREAS,   on July 24, 2023, an action in lieu of   prerogative writ captioned   “ In re Township of West  Orange ” , ESX-L-004687-23, was filed in the Superior Court of New Jersey, Law Division, Essex County  by the Mayor of West Orange seeking relief from certain actions and inactions of the Township Council;  and  WHEREAS , when the Mayor and Council are adversaries in the same litigation each requires their own  counsel to avoid conflicts and as stated by the N.J. Supreme Court in the case of Municipal Council v.  James, 183 N.J. 361 (2005); and  WHEREAS , the Township Council of the Township of West Orange has determined by vote taken in an  open public meeting to retain the legal services of Angelo J. Genova, Esq., and his firm, Genova Burns,  494 Broad Street, Newark, NJ 07102, to represent its interests in the aforesaid law suit; and  WHEREAS , the terms of such representation are set forth in an Agreement Regarding Legal Services  ( “ the Agreement ” ) dated July 28, 2023 and attached hereto together with letter of transmittal of the same  date as Exhibit   “ A ” , which provide for a cap or not to exceed amount of $17,499.00 any expenditure  beyond which amount requires Council approval; and  WHEREAS , the Township Council of the Township of West Orange wishes to authorize an additional  expenditure in the amount of $24,994.50 subject to confirmation of available funds by the Township ’ s  Chief Financial Officer; and  WHEREAS , Genova-Burns, LLC has completed and submitted a Business Entity   Disclosure Certification  which certifies that Genova - Burns, LLC has not made any reportable   contributions to a candidate  committee in the   Tow nsh ip of West Orange   in the previous one year,   and that the contract will prohibit  the   Genova Burns , LLC   from making any reportable   contributions   through the term of the contract, and  NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT AND IT IS HEREBY RESOLVED BY THE TOWNSHIP COUNCIL OF  THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE , that Angelo J.   Genova, Esq., and the firm of Genova Burns  LLC appropriation allocated is increased by $24,994.50.  BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED   that the Business Disclosure Entity   Certification and the Determination  of Value be placed on file with this resolution and published in   the   West Orange Chronicle.  Karen J.   Carnevale, R.M.C.   Bill R utherford, Council President  Municipal   Clerk  Adopted: January 9, 2024  I certify funds are available for this work   fro m   Account No .  John O. Gross, Chief Financial Officer

36-24  January 9, 2024  R E S O L U T I O N  WHEREAS,   County   Route   659   (Main   Street)   is   a   densely   developed   and   thriving   residential   and  business   corridor   traversing   the   Township   of   West   Orange   with   significant   pedestrian   and   bicycle   traffic;  and  WHEREAS ,   County   Route   659   (Main   Street)   and   the   adjacent   streets   have   a   mix   of   business   and  residential uses and it is heavily travelled by pedestrian traffic on a daily basis ; and  WHEREAS,   Mayor   McCartney   and   the   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of   West   Orange   are  deeply concerned about pedestrian safety along   County   Route 659 (Main Street) ; and  WHEREAS,   the   West   Orange   Engineering   Department   and   the   Essex   County   Department   of  Public   Works   and   Engineering   Division   determined   that   the   crosswalks   at   the   intersection   of   Franklin  Avenue   and   Main   Street ,   Charles   Street   and   Main   Street   and   Northfield   Avenue   near   St.   Mark ’ s   Church  are in need of an emergency repair ; and  WHEREAS ,   pursuant   to   the   Local   Public   Contracts   Law,   Emergency   Contracts,   N.J.S.A.  40A:11.6a   the   Assistant   Municipal   Engineer   on   November   8 th ,   2023   and   December   5 th ,   2023   notified   the  Administration   and   Purchasing   Agent   of   the   need   to   perform   an   emergency   contract   for   this   work   with   a  total   estimated cost of $ 65 ,000.00   to $100,000 .00   which is above the bid threshold ; and  WHEREAS,   it   was   concluded   that   to   expedite   the   work   and   to   perform   the   emergency   repair   of  the   crosswalks   at   the   intersection   of   Franklin   Avenue   and   Main   Street ,   Charles   Street   and   Main   Street   and  Northfield   Avenue   near   St.   Mark ’ s   Church   to   protect   public   health   and   safety ,   an   emergency   contract  would   be   issued   to   Essex   &   Morris   Contracting   Co. ,   12   Candice   Way,   East   Hanover,   NJ   07936   and   was  readily available to perform the work ; and  WHEREAS,   Essex   &   Morris   Contracting   Co.   submitted   a n   invoice   dated   December   22 ,   2023   for  the   emergency   repair   for   the   crosswalks   at   the   intersection   of   Franklin   Avenue   and   Main   Street,   Charles

Street   and   Main   Street   and   damaged   brick   paver   walkway   in   front   of   No.   429   Main   Street   in   the   amount  of   $56,282.92 ; and  WHEREAS ,   the   Assistant   Municipal   Engineer   by   letter   dated   January   3,   2024   recommends  paying the costs of this emergency work; and  WHEREAS , sufficient funds exist for this work; and  WHEREAS , the Township Council concurs in the foregoing .  NOW   THEREFORE   BE   IT   RESOLVED   by   the   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of   West  Orange   that   an   emergency   appropriation   in   an   amount   $ 56 ,282.92   is   authorized   to   Essex   &   Morris  Contracting   Co.,   12   Candice   Way,   East   Hanover,   NJ   07936   for   emergency   repair   of   the   crosswalks   at   the  intersection   of   Franklin   Avenue   and   Main   Street ,   Charles   Street   and   Main   Street   and   damaged   brick   paver  walkway in front of No. 429 Main Street .  BE   IT   FURTHER   RESOLVED   that   this   work   was   performed   pursuant   to   the   provisions   of   the  Local   Public   C ontracts   Law,   Emergency   Contracts ,   N.J.S.A.   40A:11-6   as   the   work   was   required   t o  protect public health and welfar e. 37-24  Jan u ary 9, 2024  R E S O L U T I O N  A CCEPTING THE PUBLIC IMPROVEMENTS, INCLUDING THE RIGHT S   OF WAY AT  C OLONIAL WOODS NORTH AND EXTENSION OF NANCE ROAD AND  PUBLIC EASEMENTS SHOWN ON THE FILED MAP ENTITLED  “ FINAL PLAT-BLOCK 173, LOT 2AND BLOCK 172.29, LOT 40.02, COLONIAL WOODS  NORTH, TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE, ESSEX   COUNTY, NEW JERSEY ”  WHEREAS ,   RCN   Developers ,   LLC,   ( “ Developer ” )   entered   into   a   Developer ’ s   Agreement  with   the   Township   of   West   Orange   ( “ Township ” )   by   Resolution   No.   18-09   dated   January   20,   2009 ;  and  WHEREAS,   a   cash   bond   in   the   amount   of   $178,620.00   were   submitted   to   the   Township   by  the   Developer   to   guarantee   completion   of   site   improvements   to   be   performed   in   accordance   with   the  preliminary   and   final   major   subdivision   approval   from   the   Planning   Board   of   the   Township   and   said  approval was memorialized by resolution adopted by the Board on August 7, 2003; and  WHEREAS,   the   Township   Engineer   has   determined   that   the   public   improvements   associated  with this project have been completed and found to be satisfactory; and  WHEREAS,   the   Assistant   Township   Engineer,   per   correspondence   dated   December   6,   2023,  has   recommended   a   Maintenance   Bond   in   the   amount   of   $117,964.44,   and   for   the   time   period   of   two  (2) years from the date of acceptance of the improvements by the Township; and  WHEREAS,   the   Assistant   Township   Engineer,   per   correspondence   dated   December   6,   2023,  has   recommended   that   a   payment   to   the   Township   be   submitted   in   the   amount   of   $23,275.00   for   the  Township to plant   the 49   trees that were not planted as part of the Developer ’ s   Agreement;   and  WHEREAS,   the Township Council concurs in the foregoing.  NOW   THEREFORE   BE   IT   RESOLVED   by   the   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of  West Orange,   in the County of Essex and State of   New Jersey ,   as follows:  1.   A ll   the   public   improvements   within   the   rights   of   way   of   Colonial   Woods   North   and   extension  of   Nance   Road   and   public   easements   shown   on   the   filed   map   entitled   “ FINAL   PLAT-  BLOCK   173,   LOT   2AND   BLOCK   172.29,   LOT   40.02,   COLONIAL   WOODS   NORTH,  TOWNSHIP   OF   WEST   ORANGE,   ESSEXCOUNTY,   NEW   JERSEY ”   and   filed   in   the

office   of   the   Clerk   of   Essex   County   on   July   30,   2007   as   Filed   Map   No.   4518   are   hereby  accepted   by the Township of West Orange.  2.   Maintenance   Bond   in   the   amount   of   $117,964.44   and   Tree   Payment   in   the   amount   of  $23,275.00   shall   be   deducted   from   the   cash   bond   currently   held   by   the   Township   in   the  amount of $178, 620.00.  3.   Therefore, the remaining balance of $37,380.56 is hereby released to RCN Developers, Inc.  BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED   that   the Township Clerk is hereby directed to transmit  certified copies of this resolution to the following individuals and entities   RCN Developers , LLC,  Township Engineer, Chief Financial Officer, and Township Attorney.  Karen   J.   Carnevale,   R.M.C.,   Municipal Clerk   Bill Rutherford , Council   President  Adopted: January 9, 2024  I hereby certify funds are available   from   Account:  $37,380.56  John Gross, Chief Financial Officer

documents.title: RESOLUTION RE ANTISEMITISM 4869 - 7312 - 9882 V.1

pages.text: 39-24  January 9, 2024  RESOLUTION TO CONFRONT, CONDEMN AND COMBAT ANTI SEMITISM  WHEREAS ,   individu als   looking   to   promote   hate   in   the   United   States   have   historically  exploited   and   continue   to   exploit   bigotry   and   weaponize   hate   for   political   gain,   targeting  traditionally   persecuted   peoples,   including   African   Americans,   Latinos,   Native   Americans,   Asian  Americans   and   Pacific   Islanders   and   other   people   of   color,   Jews,   Muslims,   Hindus,   Sikhs,   the  LGBTQ   community,   immigrants,   and   other   marginalized   groups   with   ver bal   attacks,   incitemen t,  and   violence; and;  WHEREAS ,   harassment   on   the   basis   of   a ctual   or   perceiv ed   Jewish   ancestry,   ethnicity,  identity,   affiliation,   or   faith,   remains   a   pervasive   and   disturbing   problem   in   contemporary  American society and fundamentally contradicts the   values of our West Orange community; and  WHEREAS ,   antisemitism,   violates   the   basic   principles   of   tolerance,   pluralism,   and  democracy, and the shared values   that bind Americans of all backgrounds together; and  WHEREAS ,   incidents   of   antisemiti sm   in   the   United   State s,   including   in   the   state   of   New  Jersey, have greatly incre ased   in recent years as evidenced by the following:  •   In   2022,   The   Anti-Defamation   League ’ s   ( “ ADL   '')   Audit   of  Antisemitic   Incidents   tabulated   3,697   antisemitic   incidents   across  the   United   States.   This   represents   a   36%   increase   from   the   2,717  incidents   recorded   in   2021   and   is   the   highest   number   on   record  since ADL began tracking antisemitic incidents in 1979.  •   According   to   the   Federal   Bureau   of   Investigation   (the   “ FBI ” )  antisemiti c   hate   crimes   rose   25%   in   2022   from   2021.   The   FBI   also  reported   that   the   number   of   reported   single-bias,   anti-Jewish   hate  crimes   rose   from   817   incidents   in   2021   to   1,122   in   2022   and  comprised   more   than   50%   of   all   religion-based   hate   crimes   in  2022,   despite   Jews   only   accounting   for   2%   of   the   population   in   the  US;  •   Between   October   7,   2023   and   December   7,   2023,   ADL   recorded   a  total   of   2,031   antisemitic   incidents,   up   from   465   incidents   during  the   same   period   in   2022,   representing   a   337%   increase   year-over-  year;

4835-7218-2502, v. 1  WHEREAS , discriminat ion on the basis of race, religion, and ethnicity is unlawf ul under  state and federal law; and  WHEREAS , antisemitism, including harassment on the basis of actual or perceived  Jewish origin, ancestry, ethnicity,   identify, affiliation, or faith, remains a persistent, pervasive,  and disturbing problem in West Orange.;  WHEREAS , in light of the   above-mentioned   facts and danger to the Jewish community,  this Council finds it necessary to   adopt, give effect to, and combat incidents of a workab le  definition of Antis emitism;  WHEREAS   the International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance   ( “ I HRA ” )   definition  helps clarify what is antisemitic ;  WHEREAS   the IHRA definition of antisemitism reads as follows:  Antisemitism is a certain perception of Jews, which may be expressed as hatr ed  toward Jews. Rhetorical and physical   manifestations of antisemitism are directed  toward Jewish   or non-Jewish individuals and/or their property, toward   Jewish  community institutions and religious facilities.  To guide IHRA in its   work, the following ex amples may serve as illustrations:  Manifestations   might   include   the   targeting   of   the   state   of   Israel,   conceived   as   a  Jew ish ,   collectively.   However,   criticism   of   Israel   similar   to   that   leveled   against  any   other   country   cannot   be   regarded   as   antisemitic.   Antisemitism   frequently  charges   Jews   with   conspiring   to   harm   humanity,   and   it   is   often   used   to   blame  Jews   for   "why   things   go   wrong."   It   is   expressed   in   speech,   writing,   visual   forms  and action, and employs sinister stereotypes and negati ve character traits.  Co ntemporary examples of antisemitism in public life, the media, schools, the  workplace, and in the religious sphere   could , ta king into account the overall  context, include, but are not limited to:  •   Calling   for,   aiding,   or   justifying   the   killing   or   harming   of   Jews   in  the name of a radical ideology or an extremist view of   religion.  •   Making   mendacious,   dehumanizing,   demonizing,   or   stereotypical  allegations   about   Jews   as   such   or   the   power   of   Jews   as   collective   -  such   as,   especially   but   not   ex clusively,   the   myth   abo ut   a   world  Jewish   conspiracy   or   of   Jews   controlling   the   media,   economy,  government or other societal institutions.  •   Accus ing   Jews   as   a   people   of   being   responsible   for   real   or  imagined   wrongdoing   committed   by   a   single   Jewish   person   or  group, or even for acts committed by non-Jews.

4835-7218-2502, v. 1  •   Denying   the   fact,   scope,   mechanisms   (e.g.   gas   chambers)   or  intentionality   of   the   genocide   of   the   Jewish   people   at   the   hands   of  National   Socialist   Germany   and   its   supporters   and   accomplices  during World War II   (the Holocaust).  •   Accusi ng   the   Jews   as   a   people,   or   Israel   as   a   state,   of   inventing   or  exaggerating the Holocaust.  •   Accusing   Jewish   citizens   of   b eing   more   loyal   to   Israel,   or   to   the  alleged   priorities   of   Jews   worldwide,   than   to   the   interests   of   their  own nations.  •   Denying   the   Jewish   people   their   right   to   self-determination,   e.g.,  by   claiming   that   the   existence   of   a   State   of   Israel   is   a   racist  endeavor.  •   Applying   double   standards   by   requiring   of   it   a   behavior   not  expected or demanded of any other democratic nati on.  •   Using   the   symbols   a nd   images   associated   with   classic   antisemitism  (e.g.,   claims   of   Jews   killing   Jesus   or   blood   libel)   to   characterize  Israe l or   Israelis.  •   Drawing   comparisons   of   contemporary   Israeli   policy   to   that   of   the  Nazis.  •   Holding   Jews   collect ively   responsible   for   actions   of   the   state   of  Israel.  Antisemitic discrimination is the denial to Jews of opportunities or services  available to others and is illegal in many   countries.  WHEREAS ,   the   Township   acknowledges   the   following   attacks   which   were   horrif ic   and  impact   all   perso ns :   (i)   the   Tree   of   Life   Synagogue   in   Pittsburgh   on   October   2 7,   2018 ;   (ii)   the  Chabad   of   Poway   in   San   Diego   on   April   27,   20 19 ;   (iii)   the   Kosher   supermarket   in   Jersey   City   on  December   10,   2019 ;   and   ( iv)   a   Chanukah   gathering   in   Monsey ,   New   York   on   December   28,  2019 .   As   a   result,   all   houses   of   faith   especially   West   Orange   synagogues   were   compelled   to   re-  evaluate their own security measures to keep their congregations safe;  WHEREAS ,   swastikas   have   been   found   on   the   sidewalk   in   front   of   Temple   B ’ nai  Shalom   whi ch   were   vi gorously   investigated   by   the   West   Orange   Police   Department   as   well   as   on  the   bridge   at   South   Mountain   Arena   and   in   many   public   schools   in   and   around   West   Orange.   In  2023,   a   Livingston   Board   of   Education   school   bus   was   stolen   and   the   suspect   found   to   have   a  journal   that   contained   notes   about   jihad   and   other   language   against   Jewish   people   and   law

4835-7218-2502, v. 1  enforcement;   and   further   in   Bloomfield   at   Temple   Ner   Tamid   a   Molotov   cocktail   was   hurled   at  the   doors   of   their   synagogue.   In   1971 ,   a   bomb   was   planted   and   exploded   in   the   sanctuary   of   the  then   West   Orange   Jewish   Center   (now   Temple   B ’ nai   Shalom).   All   of   these   acts   have  detrimen tally   impacted   our   community   and   must   be   taken   as   an   opportunity   to   teach   about   these  issue s   and seek paths for healing and eliminating such hate;  WHEREAS ,   the   Township   of   West   Orange   has   a   responsibility   to   protect   citizens   from  acts of hate and bigotry, including antisemitism, and must be given the tools to do so ;  WHEREAS,   Alisa   Drive   in   the   Township   of   West   O range   next   to   the   West   O range   High  School   P arking   L ot   has   been   named   in   honor   of   Al i sa   M.   Flatow,   a   West   Orange   resident   who  was killed in a suicide bombing attack in Gaza   on April 9,   1995 .  NOW,   THEREFORE,   BE   IT   RESOLVED   by   the   Township   Co uncil   of   the   Township  of   West   Orange,   County   of   Essex,   State   of   New   Jersey   that   the   Township   confront   and   denounce  antisemitism,   discrimination,   racism,   hate,   and   violence   in   all   of   its   manifestations   and   stand   in  solidarity with our Jewish community members.  BE   IT   FURTHER   RESOLVED   that   the   Township   Council   affirm s   the   Township ’ s  commitment   to   cultivating   an   inclusive,   safe,   and   just   society   and   culture   that   values   the   diversity  of   our   community,   works   to   ensure   equit able   opportunities   in   all   major   facets   of   society,   and  celebrates both our individuality and commonality.  Karen J. Carnevale,   R.M.C.   Bill Rutherford  Municipal Clerk   Council President  Adop te d :   January   9, 20 24

1  Ordinance 2833-23  AN ORDINANCE AMENDING CHAPTER   21 , SECTION   ___   OF THE REVISED  GENERAL ORDINANCES OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  BE   IT   ORDAINED   BY   THE   MUNICIPAL   COUNCIL   OF   THE   TOWNSHIP   OF  WEST   ORANGE,   NEW   JERSEY   that   Chapter   21   of   the   Revised   General   Ordinances   of   the  Township of West Orange be and   are   hereby amended as follows:  I.   PURPOSE  The   purpose   of   this   ordinance   is   to   prohibit   the   use   of   gas-powered   leaf   blowers   within  the   Township   seasonally   and   then   fully   on   January   1,   2026 .   The   Township   has   deter mined   that  health   and   well-being   of   residents   and   workers   and   the   overall   climate   supports   the   provisions  herein .  II.   CHAPTER   21 ,   SECTION   ____   ENTITLED   DEFINITIONS   BE   AND   IS   HEREBY  AMENDED AS FOLLOWS :  Electric   P owered   L eaf   B lower   shall   mean   a   piece   of   equipment   powered   by   an   electric   motor  used in yard maintenance.  Gas- P owered   L eaf   B lower   shall   mean   a   piece   of   equipment   powered   by   a   two-stroke   or   four-  stroke   engine   using   gasoline   or   a   mixture   of   gasoline   and   oil   blend   as   fuel   and   used   primarily   fo r  but   not   exclusively   to   blow   grass   clippings   and   debris,   leaves,   dirt,   etc.   often   used   in   yard   and  lawn maintenance.  Turbine   B lower   commonly   referred   to   as   a   “ Buffalo   Blower ”   shall   mean   a   piece   of   equipment  that   is   connected   to   a   small   tractor,   golf   cart   or   other   vehicle   and   is   intended   to   maintain   large  parcels of land.  III.   CHAPTER   21 ,   SECTION   ___e   ENTITLED   PROHIBITED   CONDUCT   BE   AND   IS  HEREBY AMENDED AS FOLLOWS :  The   use   of   G as- P owered   L eaf   B lowers,   except   a   T urbine   B lower,   shall   be   prohibited   within   the  Township of West Orange on residential, commercial and   T ownship properties   as follows :  1.   Gas   P owered   L eaf   B lowers   may   be   permitted   March   1 5   through   April   30,   2024;  and October   1   through   December 31 , 2024.  2.   Gas   P owered   L eaf   B lowers   may   be   permitted   March   15   through   April   30,   202 5 ;  and October   1   through   December 31 , 202 5 .  3.   Effective   January   1,   2026 ,   G as- P owered   Leaf   B lowers   shall   be   prohibited  anywhere in the   T ownship for any reason.  4.   All   L e a f   B lowers   shall   be   prohibited   f rom   use   in   the   application   of   chemicals   or  products to treat bug or insect infestation.

2  5.   Hours   of   usage   for   Leaf   Blowers   shall   be   subject   to   the   same   limitations   as   set  forth in the Township of West Orange Municipal Code § 4-4.2(f).  6.   Turbine blowers shall be permitted within the Township.  IV .   CHAPTER   21 ,   SECTION   _____   ENTITLED   EX CEPT IONS   BE   AND   IS   HEREBY  AMENDED   TO ADD   AS FOLLOWS :  In the event of a weather emergency or other emergent   circumstances, the Township Emergency  Management Coordinator shall be permitted to modify the restrictions of this Ordinance.  V .   CHAPTER   21 ,   SECTION   ____   ENTITLED   PENALTIES   BE   AND   IS   HEREBY  AMENDED   TO ADD   AS FOLLOWS :  F ines   for   violation   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   applicable   to   both   the   property   owner   and   any  contractor   doing work in the Township   and shall be fixed by Resolution.  VI.   CHAPTER   21,   SECTION   ___   BE   AND   IS   HEREBY   AMENDED   TO   ADD   AS  FOLLOWS:  Landscaper Registry  All   landscapers   working   within   the   Township   shall   be   required   to   register   with   the   Department  of   Public   Works   no   later   than   March   15,   2024   and   renewed   annually   ther eafter.   A   list   of  registered   landscapers   shall   be   accessible   on   the   Township   website.   The   fee   for   registration   shall  be fixed by Resolution.  VII.   CHAPTER   21,   SECTION   ___   BE   AND   IS   HEREBY   AMENDED   TO   ADD   AS  FOLLOWS:  Violations and Penalties :  As of the effective date of this   Chapter   through   and   including   December 31, 2025, any  person or entity violating the provisions of this Chapter shall be fined as follows, and the  violator may be subject to a loss   of license to perform work within the Township of West  Orange:  A. First Offense: Written warning  B. Second Offense: $100  C. Third Offense: $200

3  Beginning January 1, 2026,   a ny person or entity violating the provisions of this chapter  shall be fined as follows, and the violator will be   subject to a loss of license to perform  work within the Township of West Orange:  A. First Offense: $500  B. Second Offense: $1,000  C. Third Offense: $1,500  VI I I .   REPEAL OF CONFLICTING ORDINANCES  Any   Ordinances   of   the   Township   which   are   in   conflict   with   this   Ordinance   are   hereby  repealed to the extent of such conflict.  IX .   SEVERABILITY  If   any   part   of   this   Ordinance   shall   be   deemed   invalid,   such   determination   shall   only   apply  to   those   specific   section(s),   paragraph(s),   subparagraph(s),   clause(s)   or   pr ovision(s)   and   those  parts   shall   be   severed .   T h e   invalidity   thereof   shall   not   a ffect   the   remaining   parts   of   this  Ordinance   which shall be deemed valid and effective. .

4  IX .   EFFECTIVE DATE  This   Ordinance   shall   take   effect   upon   final   passage   and   publication   in   accordance   with  the law.  SUSAN McCARTNEY, MAYOR  BILL RUTHERFO RD  COUNCIL PRESIDENT  KAREN J. CARNEVALE, R.M.C.,  MUNICIPAL CLERK  INTRODUCED:   December   19 , 2023  ADOPTED:   January 9, 2024  Approved as to form on the basis of facts provided.

5  2833-23  Legislative History  This Ordinance will prohibit the use of gas-powered leaf blowers within the Township of West  Orange seasonally and then fully within a designated time period.  WHEREAS,   West Orange has determined that landscaping workers require an amendment to  protect and promote the health and well-being of such workers; and  WHEREAS , the science has shown that the noise, particulate matter, gas and oil released  unburned, and the fumes from burnt fuel are dangerous to all residents and landscape wor kers.  The use of gas-powered blowers has been directly linked to hearing loss, cancers, dementia,  cardiac disease and acute stress. According to the National Academy of Science published in  National Institute of Health (NIH) gas powered blowers emit a   “ cocktail of gasses, solids, and  liquid particles ”   with small particulate matter be ing of the most concern. “ Particles may also  afflict the brain via the bloodstream … The immune system can react to particles in the lung or  bloodstream, too, triggering widespr ead inflammation that affects the brain. ”   and  WHEREAS ,   the same noise, fuels, particulate matter, and high velocity winds damage and  destroy animal, bird and insect habitats disrupting nature and adversely impacting the health of  our pets causing respiratory and other health issues; and  WHEREAS , the EPA states that operating a gas-powered leaf blower for one hour equals the  emissions of driving a gas-powered car 1100 miles.  WHEREAS,   leaving leaves in landscape beds wherever possible is essential to the   survival of  beneficial insects;  WHEREAS , mulching leaves and grass clippings generates new soil and provides natural  fertilizer to lawns and beds;  WHEREAS , the Township wishes to continue to strengthen its commitment to public health,  wellness and environmental sustainability and believes that the proposed amendments will help  achieve greater equity and public health; and  WHEREAS ,   commercial grade battery powered leaf blowers and leaf sweepers have become  more readily available, accessible and affordabl e; and  WHEREAS , the Township believes that the passage of this ordinance will more clearly reflect  the needs and values of the West Orange community.

2834-23  AN ORDINANCE AMENDING AND SUPPLEMENTING CHAPTER 7, TRAFFIC,  SUBSECTION 7-19, THROUGH STREETS   AND 7-21 MULTIWAY STOP INTERSECTION S  OF THE REVISED GENERAL ORDINANCES OF THE TOWNSHIP OF WEST ORANGE  WHEREAS , Joseph A. Fishinger, Jr., P.E., P.T.O.E and John J. Jahr P.T.P, T.S.O.S of Bright  View Engineering, have prepared an All-Way Stop Analysis Report   dated December 28,2023 which  established a warrant for All-Way Stop   Control to   be installed at the intersection of Valley Road and  Mitchell Street due to   sight distance limitations, and to prevent turning conflicts; and  BE IT ORDAINED , by the Township Council of the Township of West Orange, in the County  of   Essex, State of New Jersey,   as follows:  Section 1.   The   Township   Council   of   the   Township   of   West   Orange   finds   it   in   the   interest   of  public   safety   to   designate   the   intersection   of   Valley   Road   and   Mitchell   Street   as   an   All-Way- Stop  Intersection pursuant to N.J.S.A.39:4-8 , 39:4-138 and 39:4-197 .  Section 2.   The   Assistant   Municipal Engineer   recommends the   installation   of all -way-stop signs at  the intersection of Valley Road and Mitchell Street consistent with the current standards prescribed by the  Manual on Uniform Traffic Control Devices for Streets and Highways (MUTCD) .  Section 3.   Subsection 7-19, Through Streets, of the Revised General Ordinances of the Township  of West Orange is hereby supplemented and amended as follows pursuant to the provisions of N.J.S.A.  39: 4-140 ( italic type denotes deletion ,   bold type denotes addition ):  Name of Street   Limi ts:  Valley Road   e. The Intersection of Valley Road and  Mitchell Street is hereby designated a Multi-  Way Stop   Intersection. Stop Signs   sh all be  installed at all Intersecting Streets

Section   4 .   Subsection 7-21, Multi-Way Stops, of the Revised General Ordinances of the  Township of West Orange is hereby supplemented and amended as follows pursuant to the provisions of  N.J.S.A. 39:4-140   (italic type denotes deletion ,   bold type denotes addition ):  Intersections   Stop Signs On:  Valley Road and Mitchell   Street   All Intersecting Streets  Section   5 .   If any provisions of the Ordinance or application   thereof, under any  circumstances, is held invalid, the invalidity shall not affect any of the provisions or applications of this  Ordinance that can be given effect without the valid provision(s) or application and to this end, the  provisions of the Ordinance are severable.  Section   6 .   All other Ordinances or parts of Ordinances inconsistent with this Ordinance are  hereby repealed.  Section   7 .   Unless anothe r penalty is expressly provided by New Jersey Statute, every person  convicted of a violation of any provision of this Ordinance or any supplement thereto shall be liable to the  penalty of not more   than   fifty dollars ($50.00) or imprisonment for a term not to exceed fifteen (15) days  or both, as per W.O.R.G.O. Chapter 7-4.2  Susan McCartney , Mayor   Bill   Rutherford , Council President  Karen J. Carnevale, R.M.C., Municipal Clerk  Introduced:   January 9, 2024  Adopted:   January 23, 202 4

2834-24  Legisl a tive History  The   Ordinance,   which   amends   the   Traffic   Regulations   of   the   Township,   designates   the  intersection   of   Valley   Road   and   Mitchell   Street   as   a   Multi-Way   Stop   i ntersection   with   Stop   s igns  on   all   intersecting   streets .   Due   to   the   limited   sight   distance s   at   the   intersection,   turning  movements ,   and,   volume   of   vehicles   and   pedestrian   traffic ,   the   Township's   Engineering  D epartment   determined   that   traffic   needed   to   be   regulated   on   all   approaches   for   the   saf ety   of   the  public.   Currently   the   intersection   is   regulated   by   Stop   Signs   but   it   is   not   a   Multi   Way   Stop  i ntersection.

49-2 4  January 23, 2024  STATE OF NEW JERSEY  DEPARTMENT OF ENVIRONMENTAL PROTECTION  GREEN ACRES PROGRAM  ENABLING RESOLUTION for SUPPLEMENTAL FUNDING REQUEST  WHEREAS, the New Jersey Department of Environmental Protection, Green Acres Program  ( “ State ” ), provides loans and/or grants to municipal and county governments and grants to  nonprofit organizations for assistance in the acquisition and development of lands for outdoor  recreation and conservation purposes; and  WHEREAS, the Township of West Orange has previo usly obtained a Green Acres loan of $0.00  and/or a Green Acres grant of $3,450,000.00 from the State [and an Urban Parks grant of $0.00  from the State] to fund the following project(s):  #0722-03-005- West Orange Township Open Space Acquisition   (Rock Spring Golf Course) ;  and  WHEREAS, the Township of West Orange desires to further the public interest by requesting an  additional Green Acres loan of $0.00 and/or an additional Green Acres grant of   $ 6,300,000 .00   to  fund the project(s); and  WHEREAS, the State   shall determine if the supplemental funding request is complete and in  conformance with the scope and intent of the Green Acres Program, and notify the applicant of  the amount of the funding award; and  WHEREAS, the applicant is willing to use the State ’ s funds in accordance with its rules,  regulations and applicable statutes, and is willing to enter into an Amendment of the Agreement  with the State for the above-named project;  NOW, THEREFORE, the governing body/board resolves that:  1. Susan McCartney o r the successor to the office of   Mayor   is hereby authorized to:  (a) request such a loan and/or such a grant,  (b) provide additional information and furnish such documents as may be required, and  (c) act as the authorized correspondent of the above-named applicant; and  2. The applicant agrees to provide the local government/nonprofit share if a match is required.  3. The applicant agrees to comply with all applicable federal, state, and local laws, rules, and  regulations in its performance of the projec t.  4. This resolution shall take effect immediately.  Karen J. Carnevale,   R.M.C.   Bill R utherford , Council President  Municipal Clerk  Adopted:   January 23, 2024

WI TN ESSETH THAT:  WHEREAS,   the   Governing   Body   of   the   TOWNSHIP   has   determined   that   it   is   in   the   best  interes ts   of   the   TOWNSHIP   to   retain   the   services   of   AUDITOR   to   audit   the   regulatory   basis  financial statements of the various funds of the   TOWNS HIP;  NOW,   THEREFO RE,   in   consideration   of   the   mutual   promises,   terms   and   conditions  hereinafter set forth, the parties hereto agree as follows:  1.   The   TOWNSHIP   hereby   retains   the   services   of   AUDITOR   for   the   period   January  1,   2024   through   April 10 ,   2024   at t he   rate of $ 57 , 0 0 0 per audit.  2.   AUDITOR   is   hereby   placed   on   notice   that   no   Department   Head,   individ ual  member   of   the   Governing   Body,   or   any   agent,   servant   or   employee   of   the   TOWNSHIP   possesses  any   lawful   authority   to:   (a)   engage   the   rendition   of   service s   or   the   performance   o f   work;   (b)  authorize   the   continuation   of   services   or   work   beyond   the   amount   specifically   approved   in   the  Resolution   and   Certificate   of   Funds;   or   to   (c)   represent   that   future   funds   will   be   available   as  compensation   for   current   s ervice s. No   bills,   statement   or   vouchers   for   any

2  4849-5055-8183, v. 1  amount   exceeding   that   originally   approved   will   be   honored   or   paid   by   the   TOWNSHIP,  irrespective of whe ther   such services wer e actually performed.  3.   AUDITOR   recognizes   that   all   services   rendered   are   subject   to   the   approval   of   the  Chief   Financial   Officer,   and   that   payment   for   such   services   is   conditioned   upon   the   issuance   of   a  Purchase Order by the Towns hip en compa ssing the serv i ce s for which compensation is sought.  4.   AUDITOR   shall   submit   detailed   invoices   to   th e   TOWNSHIP   for   services  rendered   each   month   no   later   than   fifteen   (15)   days   subsequent   to   the   close   of   the   month.   All  invoices   submitted   by   AUDITOR   shall   set   forth   with   specif icity   the   tasks   performed,   the  individual performing the tasks, and the amount of time spent on each task.  5. EXISTING CONDITIONS  Multiple field   investigations   were   conducted   to   obtain   an   inventory   of existing   roadway conditions, posted traffic controls, adjacent land uses, lane configurations of the roadways in the study area, and existing vehicular and pedestrian traffic patterns. The following is a brief description of the roadways: The intersection of Valley Road & Mitchell Street is a four way unsignalized intersection with a stop control on the eastbound and westbound (Mitchell Street) approaches.  Valley Road   is a local street under the Township of West Orange which spans 1 mile between Freeman Street and Northfield Avenue. This section of the roadway is classified as an Urban Minor Collector and is under Municipal jurisdiction per the available NJDOT Straight Line Diagram ( last inventoried February 2016 ). There is no speed limit posted for this roadway. It is our opinion that a posted at 25 MPH speed limit is appropriate and conforms with the guidance set forth within the New Jersey Statutes Title 39. Valley Road provides an approximate pavement width of 30-ft and allows for bi-directional travel with one travel lane for each approach. On-street parking is permitted along both sides of the roadway south of the intersection with Mitchell Street. On street parking is prohibited along the southbound lane north of the intersection with Mitchell Street. The land use along Valley Road is generally residential in nature with neighborhood businesses at intersections.  Mitchell Street   is a local street under the Township of West Orange which spans approximately 0.74 miles between Gregory Avenue and Scotland Road. There are no NJDOT Straight-Line Diagrams available for this roadway. The speed limit on this section of Mitchell Street is posted at 25 MPH. Mitchell Street provides an approximate pavement width of 30-ft and allows for bi- directional travel with one travel lane for each approach. On-street parking is permitted along both sides of the roadway. The land use along Mitchell Street is generally residential in nature with neighborhood businesses at intersections.

DATA COLLECTION  CRASH HISTORY DATA  BVE obtained crash data through the Township of West Orange’s Police Department. We were provided with a data range from May 2018 through March 2022. These are shown in   Table 1  below:  Table 1 – Crash Data 5/18 to 3/22  DATE   STREET   CROSS STREET   TYPE   Circumstance  5/17/2018   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   Right Angle   Driver   Inattention  7/12/2018   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   Side Swipe   Driver Inattention  10/18/2018   Mitchell Street   Valley Road   Right Angle   Failed to Yield  5/8/2019   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   Right Angle   Driver Inattention  8/5/2019   Mitchell Street   Valley Road   Right Angle   Failed to Yield  10/03/2019   Mitchell Street   Valley Road   Right Angle   Driver Inattention  9/1/2021   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   Left Turn / U Turn   Improper Turning  3/14/2022   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   Head On   Field to Yield  4/8/2022   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   Rear End   Driver Inattention  Based upon the crash data provided, we observe that approximately nine (9) incidents were reported in the study area between 2018 and 2022. The reported incidents are as follows: five (5)  Right Angle , one (1)   Sideswipe , one (1)   Left Turn / U Turn,   one (1)   Head On,   and   one (1)   Rear End   . The intersection of Valley Road & Mitchell Street experienced crash frequency of approximately 2 incidents within a 12-month period. It is important to note that during the year 2020, there is a reduced frequency of crashes likely due to COVID-19 restrictions. There are less than six (6) reported crashes in a 36-month period that are correctable by an all-way stop installation. However, there are three (3) reported   “Right Angle”   crash in a 2019. Right Angle crashes are susceptible to correction by an All-Way Stop installation. It is our opinion that an All- Way Stop may be considered at the study intersection to improve the operational characteristics of the roadway.  TRAFFIC VOLUME DATA  The traffic volume data was collected using Manual Turning Movement   Counts ( MTMC ). Counts were conducted on Thursday, November 30, 2023, from 12:00AM to 11:59PM. ALL-WAY STOP ANALYSIS  We performed a All-Way STOP Analysis for the intersection of Valley Road & Mitchell Street following MUTCD guidelines. We identified Valley Road as the Major Street, which has higher traffic volumes and is classified as the Urban Minor Collector, while the local Valley Road is identified as the Minor Street. The approach speed of both the major street and minor street is 25 MPH which is less than the 40 MPH MUTCD guideline, thus the minimum vehicular warrants of at least 300 vehicles per hour for any 8 hours of an average day from the major street approach   (total of both approaches)   and at least 200 combined vehicles and pedestrian volume per hour for the same 8 hours from the minor street approach   (total of both approaches) . The volumes used for an All-Way Stop analysis is detailed in the table below:  Table 3 – Traffic Volume Analysis for Valley Road & Mitchell Street  Approach   Major   Minor   Major   Minor  Time   Valley Road   Mitchell Street   >300 MUTCD 16.1.A   >200 MUTCD 16.1.B  1   3:00PM-4:00PM   817   107   YES   NO  2   4:15PM-5:15PM   766   123   YES   NO  3   7:45AM-8:45AM   842   131   YES   NO  4   5:30PM-6:30PM   704   102   YES   NO  5   2:00PM-3:00PM   583   106   YES   NO  6   6:00AM-7:00AM   373   69   YES   NO  7   9:00AM-10:00AM   541   88   YES   NO  8   1:00PM-2:00PM   506   93   YES   NO  From this data we observe that the major approach hourly volumes do meet the hour requirements ( 300 veh ), and the minor approach do not meet the hourly volume requirements ( 200 veh ). Based on the MUTCD ( Section 2B.16 ), an All-Way Stop control is not recommended where the minor approach does not meet the requirements. Additionally, the existing roadway geometry and sight distance conditions at the study intersection are insufficient due to parked vehicles at the corner of the intersection and other objects that obstruct the view. We also observed that the two streets are of similar design and operating characteristics where an All-Way Stop control would improve the safety and operation of the intersection. Based upon these other criteria, we recommend that an All-Way Stop be implemented at the intersection of Valley Road & Mitchell Street. It is our opinion that this is appropriate based upon the existing sight distance limitations, conflicting traffic, and pedestrian safety. Our recommendations are based upon the guidance presented within section 2B.14 of the MUTCD which are outlined on the following page.

All-Way Stop Warrant Analysis Valley Road & Mitchell Street Project No.: 230143 December 28, 2023 Page 7 of 10  70 South Orange Avenue, Suite 109   PO Box 99 Livingston, New Jersey 07039   Roseland, NJ 07068 C: (732) 236-7557 T: (973) 228-0999 F: (201) 753-3904 BrightViewEngineering.com  We utilized the following criteria in evaluating the study location for the application of an All- Way STOP treatment.   Based upon MUTCD, Section 2B.12-17, All-Way Stop Control, the following justification is provided:  Section 2B.12 All-Way Stop Control  Support: 01   The provisions in the following sections describe warrants for the recommended engineering study to determine all-way stop control. Warrants are not a substitute for engineering judgment. The fact that a warrant for a particular traffic control device is met is not conclusive justification to install or not install all-way stop control. Because each intersection will have unique characteristics that affect its operational performance or safety, it is the engineering study for a given intersection that is ultimately the basis for a decision to install or not install all-way stop control. 02   All-way stop controls at intersections with substantially differing approach volumes can reduce the effectiveness of these devices for all roadway users. Guidance: 03   The decision to establish all-way stop control at an unsignalized intersection should be based on an engineering study. The engineering study for all-way stop control should include an analysis of factors related to the existing operation and safety at the intersection, the potential to improve these conditions, and the applicable factors contained in the following all-way stop control warrants: A. All-Way Stop Control Warrant A: Crash Experience (see Section 2B.13) B. All-Way Stop Control Warrant B: Sight Distance (see Section 2B.14) C. All-Way Stop Control Warrant C: Transition to Signal Control or Transition to Yield Control at a Circular Intersection (see Section 2B.15) D. All-Way Stop Control Warrant D: 8-Hour Volume (Vehicles, Pedestrians, Bicycles) (see Section 2B.16) E. All-Way Stop Control Warrant E: Other Factors (see Section 2B.17) Option: 04   The decision to install all-way stop control on site roadways open to public travel may be based on engineering judgment. Standard: 05   The satisfaction of an all-way stop control warrant or warrants shall not in itself require the installation of all-way stop control at an unsignalized intersection.  Section 2B.13   All-Way Stop Control Warrant A: Crash Experience  Option: 01   All-way stop control may be installed at an intersection where an engineering study indicates that: A. For a four-leg intersection, there are five or more reported crashes in a 12-month period or six or more reported crashes in a 36-month period that were of a type susceptible to correction by the installation of all-way stop control. B. For a three-leg intersection, there are four or more reported crashes in a 12-month period or five or more reported crashes in a 36-month period that were of a type susceptible to correction by the installation of all-way stop control.  Section 2B.14   All-Way Stop Control Warrant B: Sight Distance  Option: 01   All-way stop control may be installed at an intersection where an engineering study indicates that sight distance on the minor-road approaches controlled by a STOP sign is not adequate for a vehicle to turn onto or cross the major (uncontrolled) road. Support: 02   At such a location, a road user, after stopping, cannot see conflicting traffic and is not able to negotiate the intersection unless conflicting cross traffic is also required to stop.

All-Way Stop Warrant Analysis Valley Road & Mitchell Street Project No.: 230143 December 28, 2023 Page 8 of 10  70 South Orange Avenue, Suite 109   PO Box 99 Livingston, New Jersey 07039   Roseland, NJ 07068 C: (732) 236-7557 T: (973) 228-0999 F: (201) 753-3904 BrightViewEngineering.com  Section 2B.15 All-Way Stop Control Warrant C: Transition to Signal Control or Transition to Yield  Control at a Circular Intersection  Option: 01   All-way stop control may be installed at locations where all-way stop control is an interim measure that can be installed to control traffic while arrangements are being made for the installation of a traffic control signal (see Chapter 4C) at the intersection or for the installation of yield control at a circular intersection.  Section 2B.16 All-Way Stop Control Warrant D: 8-Hour Volume (Vehicles, Pedestrians, Bicycles)  Option: 01   All-way stop control may be installed at an intersection where an engineering study indicates: A. The combined motor vehicle, bicycle, and pedestrian volume entering the intersection from the major street approaches is at least 300 units per hour for each of any 8 hours of a typical day; and B. The combined motor vehicle, bicycle, and pedestrian volume entering the intersection from the minor street approaches is at least 200 units per hour for each of any of the same 8 hours. 02   If the 85th-percentile approach speed of the major-street traffic exceeds 40 mph, the minimum vehicular volume warrants may be reduced to 70 percent of the values given in Items A and B in Paragraph 1 of this Section.  Section 2B.17 All-Way Stop Control Warrant E: Other Factors  Option: 01   All-way stop control may be installed at an intersection where an engineering study indicates that all-way stop control is needed due to other factors not addressed in the other all-way stop control warrants. Such other factors may include, but are not limited to, the following: A. The need to control left-turn conflicts, B. An intersection of two residential neighborhood collector (through) streets of similar design and operating characteristics where all-way stop control would improve traffic operational characteristics of the intersection, or C. Where pedestrian and/or bicyclist movements support the installation of all-way stop control.

All-Way Stop Warrant Analysis Valley Road & Mitchell Street Project No.: 230143 December 28, 2023 Page 9 of 10  70 South Orange Avenue, Suite 109   PO Box 99 Livingston, New Jersey 07039   Roseland, NJ 07068 C: (732) 236-7557 T: (973) 228-0999 F: (201) 753-3904 BrightViewEngineering.com  IV.   SUMMARY & CONCLUSIONS  BVE has concluded that an All-Way Stop condition at this intersection is justified based on Section 2B.14,   All-Way Stop Control Warrant B: Sight Distance, of the 2023 MUTCD.  It is our recommendation that the Township of West Orange consider traffic calming measures and work with its residents to improve the safety and operational characteristics at the intersection of Valley Road & Mitchell Street. Based upon our observations, it is our opinion that the study intersection   warrants   based on sight distance for the implementation of an All-Way STOP Control  based on the guidelines within the MUTCD. We present the following in support of this recommendation as well as other traffic treatments which may be considered:  •   The crash data presented did not meet the requirements set forth within the MUTCD for an All-Way STOP Control ( 6 correctable incidents within a 36-month period ); however, 56% of the crashes reported were “ Right Angle ” crashes which are susceptible to correction by an All-Way Stop installation.  •   Based on the sight distance conditions observed at the intersection, it is our opinion that these conditions present a safety concern for both vehicles and pedestrians. This meets the requirements set forth within the optional guidance presented   within MUTCD for considering the implementation of an All-Way STOP control ( 2B.14–01,02 ).  •   The Township may wish to consider striping parking aisles where the roadway segment with permits to better define the travel lane width. By defining a narrower travel lane width, motorists are inclined to travel at lower speeds.  •   It is our recommendation that the Township consider implementing an international crosswalk pattern throughout all of its pedestrian crossings. This striping pattern should be comprised of white 24” thick bars, set 4’ on center, and no less than 6’ wide. This pattern provides greater visibility for both pedestrians and motorists who approach the study intersection, greater painted surface area provides greater reflectivity during nighttime lighting conditions, and due to how the pattern is laid out it may require less upkeep costs for the Township due to reduced wear and tear.  •   We recommend that the Township of West Orange provide additional speed limit signs with driver feedback throughout Valley Road & Mitchell Street.  •   We recommend additional improvements such as replacing older and faded signs, refreshing pavement striping and markings, ensuring curb ramps are ADA compliant, enhancing the landscape of the streets by maintaining the green spaces, the sidewalks, and installing additional lighting, where appropriate.

All-Way Stop Warrant Analysis Valley Road & Mitchell Street Project No.: 230143 December 28, 2023 Page 10 of 10  70 South Orange Avenue, Suite 109   PO Box 99 Livingston, New Jersey 07039   Roseland, NJ 07068 C: (732) 236-7557 T: (973) 228-0999 F: (201) 753-3904 BrightViewEngineering.com  •   We recommend that the Township of West Orange work with property owners in the study area to improve sight distance at the intersection and clear obstructions with the sight triangles, as much as is reasonable.  •   There is a sufficient amount of foot traffic in this neighborhood, it is our opinion that an All-Way Stop would improve the safety and traffic operational characteristics of the intersection. Case Status Badge # Complete Pending SEE NJTR-1B MATULLO,PATRICK 287 18037240 JACKSIC,CHRISTOPHER 137 While in the lobby of 549 Valley Rd (WOPD Traffic Bureau), I observed V1 attempt to proceed eastbound on Mitchell St through the intersection of Valley Rd. V1 then struck V2 which was travelling south on Valley Rd. The impact casued V2 to rotate 180 degrees striking V3 which was parked unoccupied on the east curbline of Valley Rd. D1 states that he stopped at the stop sign and then proceeded to travel through the intersection and did not see V2. D2 states that he was traveling south on Valley Rd when V1 came throuigh the intersection and struck V2. D1 was issued the following summonses: E18-9219 for 39:3-4 (Unregistered) and E18-9220 for 39:4-144 (Failure to stop or yield). V1 was impounded for expired registration.  3   4

Crash Description/ Narrative  D2 stated that he was driving north on Valley Rd pulling up to the Intersection of Valley Rd and Mitchell St. D2 stated he was stopped in traffic, and as he was fully stopped that is when V1 rear ended V2.  D1 stated that he was driving north on Valley Rd and had to come to a stop due to traffic at the intersection of Valley Rd and Mitchell St. He stated that due to him being in the middle of the intersection and vehicles wanting to travel east on Mitchell St, he decided to move up V1 and did not realize how close he was from the rear of V2. That is when he bumped into the rear of V2.  At the time of the crash, V1 registration was expired. The following ticket was issued 39:3-4 Driving While Unregistered (0722-E22-000784).
